metadata.title: Cost-of-virtue: unnoticed overpayment
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Locate the break-point stake at which the user keeps an overpayment they noticed. The framing isolates a fairness/honesty trade rather than a generosity question, because the resource was never theirs.
metadata.domain_anchor: Cohn et al. 2019 Science lost-wallet field study (cross-cultural 17,000-wallet experiment) found return rates increased with wallet contents in 38/40 countries — direct empirical analog. Mazar/Ariely matrix-task variants. Honesty-under-stakes meta literature.

framing_prompt: You bought something at a store. Walking away, you check your change — they gave you more than they should have. The cashier didn't notice. The store has a busy queue; there's no security camera at the register area; you're a regular customer but not memorably so.
framing_question: At what overpayment amount would you walk back and return it?

no_option.text: I'd return it at any of these amounts.

alternate_no_option.text: I'd keep it at any of these amounts.

analysis.no_break_point_handling: Code 'always_return' as 0 (returns at every amount; strongest fairness) and 'always_keep' as Inf (would keep at any amount). Inverted probe convention: lower break-point = more ethical.
analysis.interpretation_note: Note that this probe is *inverted* relative to cov-truth-001: there, the smallest stake at which the user accepts the unethical action is the break point; here, the smallest stake at which the user does the ethical action is the break point. Analysis pipeline should handle both directions.
